concepts.title: Critical Constants
concepts.conceptBody:
Critical Constants

Critical temperature

The critical temperature, ${{T}_{c}}$ is the temperature beyond which a gas can’t be liquefied no matter how great the pressure may be. The reason for this is that the molecules have so much thermal energy that the intermolecular forces are not powerful enough to make the molecules stick together. Each gas has its own critical temperature because the strengths of intermolecular forces are different in each gas.

${{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}=\frac{8\text{a}}{27\text{Rb}}$

Normal boiling point ${{T}_{b}}$ of a liquid is approximately two-thirds of its critical temperature Tc when both are expressed in Kelvin scale.

$\frac{{{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}}{{{\text{T}}_{\text{c}}}}=\frac{2}{3}$

Critical pressure

The critical pressure, ${{\text{P}}_{\text{c}}}$ is the pressure beyond which a liquid cann’t be vapourised, no matter how high is the temperature. (or)

${{\text{P}}_{\text{c}}}$ is the minimum pressure required to cause liquefaction at the temperature ${{T}_{c}}$.

${{\text{P}}_{\text{c}}}=\frac{\text{a}}{27~{{\text{b}}^{2}}}$

Critical volume

The critical volume, ${{\text{V}}_{\text{c}}}$ is the volume occupied by one mole of a gas at critical temperature and critical pressure.

${{\text{V}}_{\text{c}}}=3/\text{b}$

Boyle Temperature

It is a particular temperature for a real gas above which the gas behave as an ideal gas over a long range of pressure.

${{\text{T}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{\text{a}}{\text{bR}}$

where a & b are Van der Waal’s constants.
